summary: Gruber shares Randy Walters' story about visiting a Stanley Kubrick exhibit in Frankfurt, connecting it to his own experience at the same traveling exhibit in San Francisco. The post centers on a Kubrick quote from his 1998 DGA lifetime achievement speech, which reinterprets the Icarus myth not as a warning against ambition but as a call to engineer better solutions. Gruber ties this philosophy directly to Kubrick's legendary commissioning of a jury-rigged Mitchell BNC camera to use NASA's f/0.7 Zeiss lens for candlelight scenes in Barry Lyndon. The brief post is a meditation on craftsmanship and the refusal to accept limitations.
key_points: Kubrick's reinterpretation of the Icarus myth reframes it from a cautionary tale about hubris into an engineering challenge
The f/0.7 Zeiss lens and custom Mitchell BNC camera represent Kubrick's philosophy of solving impossible problems through better tools
Gruber draws a direct line between Kubrick's quote and his actual filmmaking practice — ambition backed by craftsmanship
money_quotes: I've never been certain whether the moral of the Icarus story should only be, as is generally accepted, 'Don't try to fly too high,' or whether it might also be thought of as 'Forget the wax and feathers, and do a better job on the wings.'
That Zeiss lens/Mitchell BNC was a better job on the wings.
key_insight: The lesson of Icarus isn't to limit your ambition — it's to build better tools worthy of it.
